frameworks.name: RFP Frame + Value Frame Stack
frameworks.definition: Every B2B sales conversation operates on two simultaneous layers: the RFP frame (commodity, price-comparison, minimum viable compliance) and the value frame (holistic business impact, ROI, total cost of risk). Winning requires satisfying the RFP floor while differentiating on the value ceiling.

frameworks.name: Case Study Close
frameworks.definition: In procurement-driven (RFP) sales contexts, attach a peer-company case study to every bid that demonstrates the most analogous client's outcome on the exact value drivers (speed, risk reduction, total cost) the buyer cares about; the prior client's voice displaces price-only comparison.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that in RFP-driven B2B service sales, the lowest-price bidder does not always win; a bidder who demonstrates superior holistic value on the dimensions the buyer actually cares about (speed of clearance, risk reduction, total-cost impact) can win at a higher price because the RFP structure limits scope but not the seller's ability to reframe the conversation.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: seller has documented differentiated outcomes on speed or risk
claims.mechanism: RFP processes train buyers to compare on price within a defined scope; by expanding the frame to total business impact before the price comparison is made, the seller shifts the evaluation criterion away from commodity attributes.
bounded_by: Requires the seller to have a relevant case study demonstrating superior outcomes
Does not apply when contractual lowest-bid requirements remove discretion

claims.text: Alex Hormozi contends that when a service business is perceived as a commodity, the single highest-leverage decommoditization move is to approach the buyer's business holistically and price against total value delivered rather than against a line-item component of the RFP scope.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: B2B service company with differentiated outcomes (e.g., speed, risk reduction)
claims.mechanism: Buyers using RFPs limit scope to commoditize competition; by demonstrating an understanding of the buyer's full system costs, the seller breaks the commodity comparison and positions against a larger value pool than peers who answer only the stated RFP.
bounded_by: Requires the seller to genuinely understand the buyer's broader business economics
Does not help if the seller has no differentiated outcomes to demonstrate

claims.text: Alex Hormozi states that in RFP-driven procurement, including a peer-company case study that shows how a comparable client achieved superior outcomes (on the seller's specific differentiators) converts the buyer's frame from price comparison to outcome comparison, increasing win probability even when the bid is not the cheapest.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: seller has a documented case study from a client comparable to the target buyer
claims.mechanism: Procurement evaluators default to price-mode when reading RFPs; a case study showing outcome-mode evidence (even from an anonymous but detailed prior client) interrupts the price-comparison heuristic and reanchors the evaluation on return value.
bounded_by: Case study must be from a client whose situation is recognizably similar to the target buyer
Anonymous case studies require sufficient meta-detail (industry, size, geography) to be credible

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that even when a prior client cannot be named by name, an anonymized case study remains effective provided the seller includes enough identifying meta-details (industry vertical, city, company size class) to establish the social proof's believability. The goal is to get as close to named proof as confidentiality allows.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Client has signed NDA or refuses public attribution
B2B service RFP or procurement context
claims.mechanism: Buyers calibrate their belief in a case study by the specificity of the social proof signal; replacing the client name with industry + location + size class (e.g., defense contractor, Baltimore, MD, 2,000 employees) preserves most of the credibility at low confidentiality risk.
bounded_by: Whitewashed proof is weaker than named proof; use named proof whenever possible

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that for buyers in a procurement mode, the opening paragraph of any sales communication (including an RFP response) should set the value frame as early as possible, before the buyer anchors on price. The frame to establish is: the buyer wants the highest ratio of dollars-in to dollars-out, not the lowest input cost, and the two are often inversely correlated.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: Written or verbal B2B sales communication
claims.mechanism: Buyers who have anchored on price before seeing value evidence will evaluate the entire pitch through a cost lens; pre-framing the evaluation criterion (ROI ratio, not unit price) before presenting numbers redirects the evaluative heuristic.
bounded_by: Does not apply when the buyer is contractually required to select the lowest bid regardless of value framing

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends that service businesses bidding on RFPs include a consulting rider: a clause offering any additional one-time consulting services at a 25% discount during the contract term. This serves as a foot-in-the-door mechanism that converts a project or contract win into ongoing consulting revenue and expands the relationship scope beyond the original RFP.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: B2B service company already responding to an RFP
Company has capacity to deliver consulting beyond the core service
Buyer has multiple service needs beyond the RFP scope
claims.mechanism: Large-company procurement processes make initial budget approval difficult but incremental scope expansion easy once the vendor relationship is active; a consulting rider pre-authorizes that expansion path at a credible discount, opening the door before the contract begins.

anchors.value: 25% off one-time consulting during contract term

bounded_by: Depends on size of target company and procurement process structure
Consulting must be deliverable by the service provider at acceptable margin

claims.text: Alex Hormozi observes that in large-company procurement, the hardest step is getting the initial budget approved and the money spigot turned on; once an approved vendor relationship exists, it is significantly easier to adjust scope upward and increase budget than it was to secure the original approval.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Budget approval processes create high initial friction (committee review, competitive bids, legal); once a vendor is approved and performing, incremental scope expansion bypasses most of that friction because internal champions can approve smaller additions without re-running procurement.
bounded_by: Depends on company size and procurement policy
Does not apply to small businesses with informal buying processes

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that a service business with no brand, no advertising, and no marketing can still build a strong company by focusing on a high-quality money model and excellent retention. Brand investment is valuable for pricing power, but it is not a prerequisite for business viability.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: you don't need a brand all the time
conditions: Primary growth through word of mouth and RFP
bounded_by: Brand becomes important when the business wants inbound deal flow and pricing power at scale
Word-of-mouth plus RFP growth has a ceiling that brand investment can eventually raise

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that treating any sales conversation as a synthetic RFP conversation increases win probability significantly, because every B2B buyer has an implicit RFP frame (minimum requirements, price expectations) and an implicit value frame (desired outcomes). Addressing both simultaneously outperforms pitches that address only one.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Buyers in B2B contexts always have a floor (RFP frame: compliance, price, vendor stability) and a ceiling (value frame: outcomes, speed, risk). Pitches that only address the floor compete on commodity attributes; pitches that address both win on differentiated value while still clearing the floor.
bounded_by: Requires the seller to actually have differentiated outcomes on the value frame dimensions the buyer cares about

claims.text: Alex Hormozi notes that high customer retention (95%+ annually) in a B2B service business is a latent pricing power asset: it is evidence that delivered value exceeds price, and can be monetized as a proof point in new sales conversations to justify premium pricing.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: High retention signals high perceived value relative to price; in a commoditized market where buyers assume parity among vendors, documented superior retention is observable evidence that the service delivers differentiated value and supports a premium pricing argument.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends that in a sales pitch or RFP response, the analogy of cheapest leads vs. highest-converting leads should be used to reframe the buyer's mindset from price minimization to return maximization: the cheapest option is rarely the one with the highest dollars-in to dollars-out ratio, and the pitch should make this explicit early.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: B2B service with measurable outcome differentiation
claims.mechanism: Abstract value claims are dismissible; a concrete analogy (you don't want cheapest leads, you want highest-converting leads) forces the buyer to apply familiar logic (ROI > cost) to their procurement decision, making the value frame feel self-evidently correct rather than a sales tactic.
bounded_by: Requires a seller who actually delivers superior ROI, not just higher price

examples.text: During a live coaching session, Alex Hormozi worked with the founder of OccuMed, an $18M top-line employment medical evaluation business serving large employers, defense contractors, and government agencies. The business had 95%+ client retention but was perceived as a commodity because it primarily sold through competitive RFPs and had done almost no marketing or advertising. Hormozi diagnosed the problem as a missing value frame: OccuMed was winning or losing on price alone because they were not proactively reframing the RFP conversation around their differentiators (speed of clearance, global network, risk reduction). His prescription was to attach a peer-company case study to every RFP response and to set the value frame in the opening paragraph, positioning OccuMed as the solution that maximizes the buyer's total return rather than the lowest per-evaluation cost.

Hormozi shared a personal credential mid-conversation to explain his familiarity with the government contracting world: his first job out of college was at a boutique strategy firm doing space, cyber, and intelligence work that consisted almost entirely of public-sector RFP contracts. This gives him first-hand experience with how procurement-driven buyers think and why the standard RFP-response tactic (answer the question, submit the price) leaves differentiation value on the table.

To illustrate anonymized proof, Hormozi gave a concrete example of how to whitewash a case study when the client will not be named: instead of saying Alex Ramosi, say AH, Baltimore, Maryland. The principle is to provide the maximum identifying detail that confidentiality allows (initials, city, industry, size class) so that the proof feels specific and verifiable rather than fabricated. He referenced a 13-point proof checklist inside Acquisition.com's sales system as the comprehensive version of this practice.

practices.text: When writing an RFP response or opening a B2B sales conversation, set the value frame in the first paragraph before the buyer anchors on price. State explicitly that the goal is the highest ratio of dollars-in to dollars-out, not the lowest input cost.

Attach a case study from the most comparable prior client to every RFP submission. If the client cannot be named, whitewash the attribution with as much identifying meta-detail as confidentiality allows (industry, city, company size class). Use the 13-point proof checklist to ensure all possible proof elements are included.

Include a consulting rider clause in RFP bids offering any additional one-time consulting services at a 25% discount during the contract term to create a foot-in-the-door for ongoing revenue expansion once the initial budget is approved.

Treat every B2B sales conversation as a synthetic RFP by simultaneously addressing both the RFP floor (minimum requirements, price compliance) and the value ceiling (total business impact, ROI on the differentiating factors the buyer actually cares about).

Identify your top two or three differentiated performance dimensions (e.g., speed, risk reduction, retention rate) and get quantified statistics that demonstrate the cost of those outcomes on a global or system-wide level, not just a per-unit price.
